festivals.name: Paris Fashion Week
festivals.description: One of the world's most prestigious fashion weeks showcasing haute couture and ready-to-wear collections.

festivals.name: Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe
festivals.description: Europe's most valuable horse race held in Paris, attracting high society and racing enthusiasts.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of France
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 15 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Prehistoric France
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers and early farmers shaped the landscape of what would become France.
items.text: After the last Ice Age, nomadic hunters roamed the valleys and coasts of present-day France, leaving behind remarkable cave paintings like those at Lascaux and Chauvet. Around 7000 BC, farming communities from the Mediterranean began spreading northward, bringing domesticated animals and crops. These Neolithic settlers built megaliths—standing stones and burial chambers—that still dot the Breton and Norman countryside. By 3000 BC, Bronze Age peoples had established trade networks across Europe, establishing the first permanent villages and social hierarchies.

items.label: Gallic Kingdoms
items.summary: Celtic tribes dominated the region, developing rich cultures but remaining fiercely independent.
items.text: Celtic peoples known as Gauls migrated into the region around 800 BC, bringing iron-working technology and a warrior aristocracy. They organized themselves into dozens of independent tribes—the Aedui, Helvetii, Arverni, and Helvetii among them—each with their own laws, coinage, and territories. These societies were sophisticated: they built oppida (fortified towns), farmed extensively, and created intricate metalwork and art. However, their lack of political unity would prove fatal when facing Rome's organized legions.

items.label: Roman Conquest
items.summary: Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars crushed Celtic resistance and incorporated Gaul into Rome's empire.
items.text: Julius Caesar launched his invasion of Gaul in 58 BC, systematically conquering tribe after tribe over eight years of brutal warfare. The turning point came at the siege of Alesia in 52 BC, where the charismatic Gallic leader Vercingetorix made his last stand against Roman forces. Though Vercingetorix was defeated and later executed, Caesar's victory was complete: Gaul became a Roman province, fundamentally altering the region's culture, language, and politics. The conquest brought roads, towns, law, and Latin—the foundation for the French language.

items.label: Roman Gaul
items.summary: Five centuries of Roman rule brought urbanization, Christianity, and cultural synthesis.
items.text: Under Roman rule, Gaul prospered as one of the empire's richest provinces. Cities like Lugdunum (Lyon), Burdigala (Bordeaux), and Lutetia (Paris) grew into centers of commerce and learning, connected by an impressive network of roads. The Gallic aristocracy gradually adopted Roman customs, language, and governance, while Celtic culture faded into memory. Christianity spread slowly from the south, becoming the dominant religion by the 4th century. As Rome's power weakened in the 5th century, Germanic tribes—Visigoths, Burgundians, and Franks—increasingly crossed the borders, eventually establishing kingdoms within the collapsing empire.

items.label: Frankish Rise
items.summary: The Franks transformed Gaul into the Frankish kingdom, laying the groundwork for medieval France.
items.text: Clovis I, king of the Salian Franks, defeated the last Roman governor of Gaul in 486, establishing Frankish dominance across the region. He unified Frankish tribes through military conquest and, crucially, converted to Catholic Christianity around 500, winning the allegiance of the Gallo-Roman population and the Church. His descendants, the Merovingian kings, gradually saw their power erode as local nobles and their administrators, the mayors of the palace, accumulated influence. The most powerful of these mayors was Charles Martel, who defeated a massive Muslim invasion at Tours in 732, securing Christian Europe's future. By 751, his son Pepin the Short deposed the last Merovingian and founded the Carolingian dynasty, promising renewed strength and order.

items.label: Carolingian Empire
items.summary: Charlemagne built a vast empire and revived learning, only to see it divided among his heirs.
items.text: Charlemagne, crowned king of the Franks in 768, spent decades conquering neighboring kingdoms and converting pagans by force, eventually creating an empire stretching from the North Sea to Rome. In 800, Pope Leo III crowned him Emperor of the Romans, reviving the title and prestige of Rome itself. He established a capital at Aachen, reformed the military, standardized writing and education, and surrounded himself with scholars—the Carolingian Renaissance. His empire brought roads, schools, and administrative unity to Western Europe. However, when Charlemagne died in 814, his empire fractured among his grandsons; the Treaty of Verdun in 843 officially divided it, with Charles the Bald receiving the western portion—the kingdom that would evolve into France.

items.label: Early Medieval Kingdom
items.summary: A weak central monarchy and powerful nobles carved up the land during centuries of Viking invasions and feudal chaos.
items.text: After Charlemagne's empire shattered, the kings of the West Frankish kingdom (Francia) found their authority constantly challenged by regional magnates and devastated by Viking raids. Norse raiders penetrated deep into the kingdom, burning cities and monasteries, until finally the king granted them Normandy in 911 to stop the invasions. The feudal system took root as nobles built castles, raised private armies, and controlled vast territories with little regard for the distant king. By the 10th century, powerful dukes—of Aquitaine, Burgundy, Normandy, and Brittany—were more powerful than the king himself. The Capetian dynasty, which took the throne in 987 under Hugh Capet, inherited a kingdom that was little more than a collection of fiefdoms, with their direct control limited to the Île-de-France around Paris.

items.label: Norman Conquest and Feudal France
items.summary: Norman kings of England held vast French lands, complicating the Capetians' struggle to consolidate power.
items.text: When William the Conqueror, Duke of Normandy, invaded and conquered England in 1066, he created a paradox: the kings of England were also powerful Norman and Angevin nobles holding vast territories in France, often more powerful than the French king himself. The Capetians slowly expanded their domain through shrewd marriages, military campaigns, and legal maneuvering. Kings like Louis VI and especially Louis VII began asserting royal authority beyond their heartland. The greatest triumph came under Philip II (Philip Augustus), who defeated the English king John and seized most of the English lands in France, dramatically expanding the crown's power. By 1328, when the last direct Capetian died, the French monarchy had grown strong enough to pass through a complex succession—but it faced a powerful rival in England's Edward III, whose claim would ignite a catastrophic war.

items.label: Hundred Years' War
items.summary: A brutal, intermittent conflict with England devastated France but ultimately forged a stronger nation.
items.text: Edward III of England, claiming the French throne through his mother, invaded France in 1337, launching a conflict that would span over a century. The English won stunning victories through superior tactics—particularly the longbow—at Crécy (1346) and Agincourt (1415), while French nobility suffered terrible losses and captivity. The war was catastrophic for the French countryside: armies ransacked villages, peasants starved, and the economy collapsed under the strain of financing the endless conflict. Yet the war also kindled French national identity; most famously, Joan of Arc, a peasant girl who heard divine voices, rallied the French in 1429 and turned the tide before her capture and execution. By 1453, the English were driven from all French territory except Calais, leaving France battered but unified under a stronger monarchy that had learned to raise armies and levy taxes.

items.label: Renaissance and Reformation
items.summary: France embraced Renaissance culture while torn apart by religious civil wars between Catholics and Huguenots.
items.text: Following the Hundred Years' War, French kings like Francis I and Henry II adopted the ideals and aesthetics of the Italian Renaissance, becoming great patrons of art, architecture, and literature. The Loire Valley filled with magnificent châteaux, and Paris became a center of humanist learning. Yet this cultural flowering was shadowed by religious conflict: Protestant reformers gained followers, creating a Huguenot minority that alarmed the Catholic establishment. The wars of religion erupted in 1562 and continued with brutal intensity for decades, culminating in the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre of 1572, when Catholics slaughtered thousands of Huguenots in Paris. After years of devastation, Henry IV, a former Protestant king, accepted Catholicism and in 1598 issued the Edict of Nantes, granting religious toleration and ending the wars—but at enormous cost to French society.

items.label: Ancien Régime
items.summary: French absolutism reached its height under Louis XIV, creating Europe's most powerful kingdom and most elaborate court.
items.text: Louis XIV, the Sun King, inherited the throne as a child and grew to embody absolute monarchy, famously declaring 'L'État, c'est moi' (I am the State). He built the Palace of Versailles into the largest and most opulent royal residence in Europe, where he imprisoned the nobility in gilded cages of court ritual and etiquette, transforming potential rivals into dependent courtiers. His long reign (1643–1715) saw France become Europe's dominant military and cultural power, though ceaseless wars drained the treasury. His successors, Louis XV and Louis XVI, proved less capable, and the monarchy's authority gradually eroded as enlightened thinkers like Voltaire and Rousseau questioned absolute power. By the 1780s, France faced bankruptcy, peasants starved, aristocrats clung to privileges, and middle-class ambitions went unmet—a powder keg awaiting ignition.

items.label: French Revolution and Napoleonic Era
items.summary: Revolutionary upheaval destroyed the old order, executed the king, and gave birth to nationalism and modern democracy—under Napoleon's dictatorship.
items.text: The French Revolution began in 1789 when starving masses stormed the Bastille, shattering the myth of royal invincibility. The National Assembly abolished feudalism, declared the Rights of Man, and attempted to create a constitutional monarchy. Yet the revolution radicalized: the king and queen were executed in 1793, the Reign of Terror saw thousands guillotined, and the nation convulsed in civil war. From the chaos emerged a brilliant military officer, Napoleon Bonaparte, who took power in 1799 and brought order, legal reform (the Napoleonic Code), and religious settlement. As emperor, Napoleon conquered most of Europe, spreading revolutionary ideals but also arousing nationalist resistance. His invasion of Russia in 1812 proved catastrophic; he was defeated and exiled in 1814, returning briefly in 1815 before final defeat at Waterloo. Despite his downfall, the Revolution had fundamentally transformed France and Europe, destroying aristocratic privilege and establishing the principles of popular sovereignty and civil law.

items.label: 19th Century: Restoration to Republic
items.summary: France cycled through monarchy, republic, and empire while becoming an industrial power and cultural superpower.
items.text: After Waterloo, the monarchy was restored, but the old order was broken beyond repair. Revolution erupted again in 1830 and 1848 as liberals and republicans demanded representative government. The Second Empire under Napoleon III (1852–1870) brought modernization and industrial growth but ended in catastrophic defeat by Prussia in 1870, costing France territory and humiliation. The Third Republic, proclaimed in 1870 and consolidating power by 1875, proved surprisingly durable despite internal conflicts, Dreyfus Affair scandals, and perpetual instability. Industrialization transformed France's economy and society, while the belle époque saw Paris become the world's cultural capital—home to impressionist painters, symbolist poets, and avant-garde artists. Despite democratic turmoil and the permanent scar of lost Alsace-Lorraine, France by 1914 was wealthy, culturally brilliant, and allied with Russia and Britain—but facing an implacable rival in an increasingly militarized Germany.

items.label: World Wars and Occupation
items.summary: Two catastrophic wars devastated France, including Nazi occupation and humiliation, before liberation and renewal.
items.text: When World War I erupted, France mobilized to reclaim lost Alsace-Lorraine and contain Germany. The war turned into a nightmare of trench warfare; the Battle of Verdun (1916) killed 300,000 men in a stalemate. France prevailed in 1918 but at shattering human cost—1.4 million dead—and the nation emerged economically weakened. The interwar years saw political instability, economic depression, and rising fascism, while a defensive military mindset produced the supposedly impregnable Maginot Line. In 1940, Germany's blitzkrieg shattered French defenses in weeks; the government surrendered and accepted Nazi occupation and a puppet regime based in Vichy. For four years, France endured oppression, collaboration, and deportation of Jews. D-Day in 1944 brought liberation, and General Charles de Gaulle restored French pride and prestige through sheer force of personality. Yet France emerged diminished: its economy was ruined, its overseas empire was unraveling, and it had lost superpower status to the United States and Soviet Union.

items.label: Modern France
items.summary: From colonial decline and political chaos, France rebuilt itself into a stable, prosperous democracy at the heart of Europe.
items.text: The Fourth Republic (1947–1958) struggled as France tried to hold onto colonial possessions—disastrously in Indochina and then Algeria, where a brutal war (1954–1962) tore the nation apart. Charles de Gaulle returned to power in 1958, granting Algerian independence and founding the Fifth Republic with a strong presidency that has endured to today. Under his leadership, France pursued an independent foreign policy, developed nuclear weapons, and gradually integrated with Western Europe. The creation of the European Union (with France as a founding member) and adoption of the euro marked France's commitment to European integration while maintaining cultural distinctiveness. Today, France is a wealthy, democratic nation of 68 million people, the world's sixth-largest economy, a permanent UN Security Council member, and home to some of humanity's greatest cultural achievements. Yet it faces modern challenges: immigration tensions, youth unemployment, regional inequality, and the rise of nationalist politics—echoes of longstanding French anxieties about identity and national purpose in an increasingly globalized world.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in France — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Stand at the bar for better prices
items.text: Coffee costs significantly less if you drink it standing at the counter (le comptoir) rather than sitting at a table. A café crème at the bar might be €1.50 while the same drink seated is €3.50 or more.

items.label: Order 'un café' for espresso, not filter coffee
items.text: When you ask for 'un café' in France, you'll get a small, strong espresso-style shot. If you want something larger, ask for 'un café crème' (with a little milk) or 'un allongé' (longer/weaker).

items.label: Coffee is for morning and after meals, not all day
items.text: French people typically drink coffee with breakfast and after lunch or dinner. Ordering multiple coffees throughout the afternoon is unusual and may draw attention.

items.label: Sit down, stay as long as you want
items.text: Once you pay for a seat, you own that table for hours if you wish—no pressure to leave. French café culture is built on lingering, reading, and observing.

items.label: Menu du jour is the daily special—best value
items.text: Lunch menus (usually €12–18) offer two or three courses at a fraction of à la carte prices. This is where locals eat, and the food is always fresh and seasonal.

items.label: Entrée means starter, not main course
items.text: French menu progression is: entrée (appetizer), plat principal (main), fromage (cheese), dessert. Don't be confused by the English word 'entrée'—it's something else here.

items.label: À la carte is expensive—use formules or prix fixe instead
items.text: Ordering individual dishes from the à la carte section costs nearly double. Most restaurants offer 'formule' (two courses) or 'menu' (three courses) at set prices.

items.label: Plat means main course, not a plate
items.text: When you see 'plat du jour' (dish of the day) or 'plat principal' (main course), you're looking at the central course, typically served with vegetables or potatoes.

items.label: Cheese course before dessert is standard
items.text: In formal French dining, fromage comes after the main and before dessert. You can skip it, but it's considered the proper progression.

items.label: Coq au vin
items.text: A Burgundy classic: chicken braised in red wine with mushrooms, pearl onions, and lardons. Rich, deeply flavored, and perfect with crusty bread to soak up the sauce.

items.label: Sole meunière or Dover sole
items.text: Whole flatfish pan-fried in butter with lemon. A coastal French staple that's simple but revelatory if prepared well—look for this especially in Brittany and Normandy.

items.label: Cassoulet
items.text: A white-bean stew from southwest France layered with duck confit, sausage, and breadcrumb topping. Hearty, warming, and best eaten in Toulouse or Carcassonne where it's taken seriously.

items.label: Bouillabaisse
items.text: A saffron-scented fish stew from Provence (especially Marseille) served with rouille and crusty bread. Authentic versions are expensive but worth trying once in a coastal city.

items.label: Steak frites with béarnaise
items.text: A simple bistro classic: quality beef steak, crispy fries, and a rich tarragon-butter sauce. French beef quality is excellent, and this is how locals eat meat casually.

items.label: Croque monsieur and croque madame
items.text: A ham and cheese sandwich grilled in butter (monsieur), or with a fried egg on top (madame). A café lunch staple that's far better than it sounds when made properly.

items.label: Brasseries are reliable for lunch and late hours
items.text: Brasseries serve straightforward bistro fare throughout the day and into late evening, making them ideal for travelers who don't eat on a French schedule. Look for the brass rail and ornate mirrors—the name comes from 'brasseur' (brewer).

items.label: Avoid restaurants directly facing major tourist attractions
items.text: Restaurants with a view of the Eiffel Tower, Notre-Dame, or a famous monument charge double and serve mediocre food. Walk two blocks away and prices drop, quality rises.

items.label: Look for 'bistro' for affordable regional cooking
items.text: True bistros are neighborhood establishments serving traditional French comfort food at fair prices. They're usually small, busy at lunch, and closed between 2–7pm.

items.label: Check reviews on TheFork app, not Yelp
items.text: TheFork (lafourchette.com) is France's primary restaurant reservation platform and shows menus, prices, and reviews from actual diners. It also offers discounts on reservations.

items.label: Market restaurants serve the best value food
items.text: Many open-air markets have small restaurant stalls or casual eateries attached. Food is sourced that morning and prices are excellent—perfect for lunch.

items.label: Wine is for meals, water and pastis are for café
items.text: Drinking wine at a café without food is unusual. Instead, order 'un verre de vin' (a glass of wine) with your meal, or try pastis (anise liqueur) as an aperitif with water.

items.label: Ask for 'un verre de rouge' or 'un verre de blanc'
items.text: Order wine by color (red/white/rosé) rather than getting stuck with an expensive bottle. A house wine by the glass is always available and usually solid quality.

items.label: Kir and Kir Royale
items.text: A traditional aperitif: white wine with blackcurrant liqueur (Kir), or champagne with blackcurrant (Kir Royale). Light, refreshing, and often cheaper than wine alone.

items.label: Champagne isn't just for celebration—it's an everyday wine
items.text: Good Champagne can be affordable in Champagne region (northeast France), but elsewhere expect to pay €40+ for a bottle in restaurants. Prosecco and Crémant are cheaper sparkling alternatives.

items.label: Tap water is safe and free—ask for 'une carafe d'eau'
items.text: French tap water is excellent and restaurants must provide it free on request. Asking for 'l'eau plate' (still) or 'l'eau pétillante' (sparkling) avoids confusion.

items.label: Digestifs like Calvados come after dessert
items.text: In traditional dining, a small glass of Calvados (apple brandy), Armagnac, or Cognac is offered after the meal to 'digest.' You can always decline, but it's part of the ritual in formal settings.

items.label: Lunch is 12–2pm, dinner starts at 8pm—eat outside these windows at your own risk
items.text: Most restaurants close between lunch and dinner service. Arriving at 3pm or 7:15pm means many kitchens are shut. Plan ahead or eat at a brasserie or café.

items.label: Make reservations, especially for dinner
items.text: Walk-ins are risky in France, even at casual bistros. Use TheFork app or call ahead—restaurants expect it and may turn away unreserved guests.

items.label: Bread is free and unlimited—but never a meal
items.text: Baskets of bread arrive automatically and cost nothing. It's meant as a side to your meal, not a substitute. Eating only bread is seen as rude.

items.label: Never ask for substitutions or modifications
items.text: French chefs cook dishes a specific way; asking to remove an ingredient or swap items is considered insulting. If you have allergies, disclose them when reserving.

items.label: Say 'Bon appétit' when people begin eating
items.text: If dining at a café or bistro and another table sits nearby, it's polite to wish them 'Bon appétit.' They'll often return the gesture.

items.label: Tipping is optional—10% for exceptional service only
items.text: Gratuity is never expected and is often not even listed on the bill. Leave a small amount (€1–2 per person or 5–10%) only if service was genuinely excellent.

items.label: Bills arrive only when requested—there's no rush
items.text: Servers will never push you out. When you're ready to leave, make eye contact and say 'L'addition, s'il vous plaît' (the bill, please) or simply say 'ça va' (that's all).

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around France.

items.label: Book in advance
items.text: SNCF tickets are cheapest 4-12 weeks ahead, especially on TGV high-speed trains. Prices spike during school holidays and weekends, so flexibility saves money.

items.label: Use the SNCF app or Trainline
items.text: Book via SNCF.com, the official SNCF app, or Trainline (cheaper outlet). Compare prices across both platforms before purchasing.
Validate your ticket before boarding by stamping it in yellow machines on platforms, or face a €50+ fine from ticket inspectors.

items.label: Consider rail passes
items.text: SNCF's Carte Liberté or a Eurail Pass may suit frequent travelers; day passes for multiple journeys can be cheaper than single tickets.
SNCF strikes (grèves) happen regularly, especially in autumn and winter, sometimes with little notice—check travel alerts before departing.

items.label: Buy a carnet or weekly pass
items.text: A carnet of 10 metro/bus tickets costs €16.90 in Paris (cheaper per-ride than singles at €2.25). Weekly passes (Navigo Découverte) or daily Tourist passes (Visite) offer better value.

items.label: Tap or validate your ticket
items.text: Hold your ticket or contactless card to the yellow reader at entry gates; many cities now accept NFC payment on phones.
Traveling without a valid ticket risks €50–150 fines from plainclothes inspectors; they are common on lines to airports and busy routes.

items.label: Download local transit apps
items.text: Use RATP (Paris), TCL (Lyon), or CTS (Strasbourg) apps for real-time schedules and service alerts; most offer trip planning and mobile tickets.
Pickpockets target crowded metro cars and tourist stations—keep bags in front and valuables secure, especially on Lines 4 and 9 in Paris.

items.label: Use official taxis or Uber
items.text: Yellow taxis are metered and regulated; Uber and Bolt operate in major cities at transparent, often cheaper rates than negotiated fares.

items.label: Book apps in advance for airport runs
items.text: Request a ride 10–15 minutes before departure to secure lower surge pricing; fixed-rate taxis from airport booths cost €30–50 to city centers.
Avoid unmarked taxis or negotiating fares in advance; unlicensed drivers often overcharge tourists, especially at airports and train stations.

items.label: Tipping is optional but appreciated
items.text: Rounding up or adding 5–10% is kind but not obligatory; card payments often have a tip option you can decline.
Airport taxi touts may aggressively solicit rides—ignore them and use official taxi queues or apps instead.

items.label: International Driving Permit & documents
items.text: Carry your valid driver's license, passport, rental agreement, proof of insurance, and an International Driving Permit (IDP) if from outside the EU—French police will request these.
Speed limits are enforced by fixed and mobile radars; exceeding limits by 1–20 km/h costs €45–90, and 20+ km/h can cost €135–750 and license suspension.

items.label: Headlights and safety gear
items.text: Dipped headlights are required at all times outside built-up areas and in poor visibility; carry a reflective vest and warning triangles—fines for missing safety equipment are €90+.

items.label: Use major rental firms or apps
items.text: Hertz, Avis, and Enterprise are reliable; always buy comprehensive insurance (CDW) to avoid large liability claims for minor damage.
Congestion charges (vignettes) apply in some city centers; check for Paris, Lyon, and Grenoble restrictions—failure to pay results in €375+ fines.

items.label: Fuel up early
items.text: Motorway service stations are expensive; fill up in towns before highway stretches or use Essence&CO app to find cheaper pumps.

items.label: Paris CDG and Orly connections
items.text: CDG has RER B trains (€11.45, 35 min to city center) and buses; Orly has Orlybus and RER C. Both have taxi ranks, but apps are often cheaper than fixed fares.

items.label: Arrive early for EU/Schengen flights
items.text: Allow 2 hours for domestic and EU flights, 3 hours for international; security lines are longest 6–9 a.m. and 4–6 p.m.
Beware of 'official' airport guides or unsolicited ride offers; book transport via official desks, apps, or pre-arranged pickups only.

items.label: Regional airports are simpler
items.text: Nice, Lyon, and Marseille airports are smaller and less crowded; shuttle buses to city centers cost €8–15 and run hourly.
Check baggage allowances and fees; budget airlines charge €5–25 per checked bag, and some airports charge parking fees for long-term dropoff areas.

items.label: Dover–Calais or Dieppe routes
items.text: P&O Ferries and DFDS operate busy routes; book in advance via their websites or ferry comparison sites for discounts, especially in off-season.

items.label: Eurostar as an alternative
items.text: London–Paris Eurostar (2 h 15 min) is faster and cheaper than ferry + driving; book at eurostar.com with significant advance discounts.
Ferry schedules change seasonally and during storms—confirm departure times and allow extra time, especially in winter.

items.label: Arrive early and bring ID
items.text: Ferry terminals require check-in 1–2 hours before departure for foot passengers, 30–45 minutes for drivers; carry your passport or ID card.

items.label: Multi-day passes in large cities
items.text: Paris Visite Pass (1–5 days, €12–65) covers metro, buses, RER, and Montmartre funicular; Lyon City Card and Marseille cards offer similar value.

items.label: Validation is crucial
items.text: Always validate single tickets or passes upon boarding by inserting them in readers; failure to do so is treated as travel without a ticket.
Ticket inspectors (contrôleurs) board transit without warning; carry proof of payment or face instant €50+ fines and potential detention.

items.label: Buy tickets at machines or booths
items.text: Metro/bus ticket machines accept cards and coins; kiosks (tabacs) often have friendlier staff and avoid long queues.

items.label: Contactless payment is widespread
items.text: Tap your contactless credit card or phone on readers instead of buying tickets; daily caps prevent overcharging.

items.label: Buy a local SIM card
items.text: Orange, SFR, and Bouygues sell prepaid plans at airports, train stations, and tabacs; unlimited data for 7–30 days costs €10–30.

items.label: Use eSIM or roaming
items.text: EU roaming works at home rates if you have a Roaming+ plan; eSIMs from Airalo or Holafly offer flexible data-only options for travelers.

items.label: Free WiFi is limited
items.text: Cafés, hotels, and some train stations offer free WiFi; RATP transit has WiFi in some Paris metro stations but it's slow—a mobile plan is essential.
Public WiFi is insecure; avoid banking or sensitive transactions on unprotected networks—use a VPN if available.

items.label: Activate phone settings before arrival
items.text: Turn off cellular data roaming before landing to avoid surprise charges; enable it only when using local SIM or confirmed roaming plans.
